commentBody: Fun fact: In addition to the ornate versions seen in the link provided in the column, ZARFS are the paper sleeves you find at Starbucks and hotel breakfast bars that your cup fits into so you don't burn your hand on the hot cup.
I hated this puzzle, but I’m mainly just brain dead tired.

This was way out of my wheelhouse.  I knew almost none of the general knowledge entries, except for ANDYKIM and NUUK, and so many of the other clues were so deliciously twisted that I thought I might have met my match.  But persistence and a few lucky guesses helped me unlock this one’s mysteries eventually.  Many thanks to Byron Walden for a Saturday worthy of the name.  LITTLEOLME thought EVENSTEVEN  for 7 up was both clever and elegant. And to top it off I learned a potentially useful scrabble word: ZARFS indeed!
Could use WordPlay column for the MIDI puzzle.

Not my cup of tea, but more because I have had a headache all day than because the puzzle was bad. I had to look a lot of stuff up.  (Including the clue that helpfully had answers from two generations ... neither guy anyone I'd heard of.)

Also I really wanted pOx instead of ROT.
What is DISS?

Never heard of EPT before. I understand the joke about it after looking it up, but that's a bit of a stretch.

I really hope we don't make it a habit of using astrological signs and their "definitions" that no intelligent person would ever know or even care about.

I was almost certain ZARFS was why I wasn't getting my gold star, but it turns out it was the INTrOACTS and rATTLED intersection. Also, having WH____ for the Marriott property for a long time made me misread W HOTEL once I got it from the crosses. I learned of them because they used to have a record label (W Records, naturally enough) that released one of my favorite Japanese Breakfast songs, "Essentially."

Wow, toughest Saturday of the year so far for me! I thought my streak was over for sure but got the happy music after a much longer time than my average. This is what a Saturday puzzle is supposed to be.

Spent 75 minutes and failed because of in two acts, not intro acts.      

Socks to not get the gold star after that.
Too much trivia. Did not like.

I read the clue for 19A [Name shared by the singer of the #1 hit "Rock Me Gently" (1974) and the first Korean American elected to the U.S. Senate (2024)] and thought "No idea who either of them is".

It wasn't until there were three letters from the crosses that it dawned on me...that's MY senator (and yes, I voted for him). D'oh!
The only thing I liked about this puzzle is… Nothing. There’s nothing I liked about this one.

Byron Walden, Byron Walden—the constructor’s name says it all. Maybe tomorrow I’ll have more to comment on but for now I’m just too tired and gobsmacked. Somehow got through this but no idea how!! You know you’re in trouble when EPT finally saves the day.
Matching overly precious clues with some real reaches made for a non solve. Honestly, who here has used KAYO?

This is one I thought I'd never finish after a first pass through all the clues. But I just kept at it and eventually made it through. I had to do a few "confirmation look-ups" where I'm pretty sure what the word is from the crossings, but don't recognize it at all (ZARFS, MÉLANGES).
Fresh clues with twists requiring diligence and stamina.  It took me 50 minutes to reach a satisfying solve.  Reminds me of the difficult Saturday puzzles of years past.
Someone please explain even Steven and 7-Up
ZARFS? Really?
Not fun. Not satisfying. When EPT is the key to wrapping up my solve, I just feel cheated.

Emulates ET? 7up? Cup holders? Up to snuff? That was really enjoyable romp Byron! It’s always fun to see one of your puzzles. I have no idea how you fit it into teaching our grouchy pre-meds Calculus!

Peace,
Elizabeth (your buddy from Biology)
Nobody born in the last 60 years has used KAYO to mean KO.

Well, this was a challenge but a good one. I was virulently anti-zarf for a few minutes but, seeing how that corner fell into place with the crosses, I'm glad it was there because I've learned an interesting word. "Intro acts" vs. "in two acts" was my biggest stumbling block. That got fixed when my solving partner/daughter figured cassowaries were more likely to be double wattled than double rattled.

This puzzle brought Saturday puzzles back to life.

— And laughed in my face, wickedly.

I got through it with some look-ups and a puzzle check (out with Mix tapES, in with MÉLANGES; out with breAkS, in with ME DAYS.)

Touché, Mr. Walden, touché, and congrats to those who sauntered through this solve or sprinted to the finish line!

Shout out to NUUK, perhaps the most beautiful city I've ever visited. The combination of Scandinavian architecture with glacial mountains in the background is unmatched. Imagine taking the houses of Nyhaven, building a whole city out of it, and putting it in the Canadian Rockies

It was actually a surfer that invented the first echolocation system

It was So Gnar

I seem to remember never being able to handle this constructor's puzzles. Today was no different. It'll be easiest if I just show you how this went for me: https://imgur.com/a/W1itkK3 Basically, much of the top half could just as well have been in Xhosa, for me. I actually remembered the W HOTEL from previous puzzles, but it was little help with how many other unknowns I encountered up there. I mean, I didn't even *know* there was a Lego movie 🤷🏽. Also, why would a friggin *gas* brand have a *torch* club? Isn't a torch one thing you do wouldn't want to have at a gas station? I had to consult a dictionary to get how forensics apply to debates. I only knew the crime lab meaning of the word. Some clues I still don't get. MANE - Trigger hair: I know what a mane is, so I get "hair", but what is "trigger" doing there? What's facetious about EPT? I'm not laughing. I got ZARFS from crosses only. That's funny because I know the thing itself well. Polish people have been huge tea drinkers for ages, and traditionally, before 1989, many drank their tea from simple, handleless glasses. You could only really use them with a zarf. By the 1980s the zarfs were cheap, plastic horrors, but previously they had been made of metal, often with quite intricate designs. These days they have been replaced by mugs to the extent that I can't actually remember what we called those zarfs decades ago. The internet tells me they were simply known as "little glass baskets", "koszyczki na szklankę".

I feel shell shocked. Did I just attempt to solve a crossword in a completely foreign language? I consider it a win when I managed to solve nearly 50% of the puzzle without looking anything up. After nearly 2 hours, I hit “reveal puzzle”, and after looking at the answers, I never in a million years would have gotten those. At least I kept my connections streak going…

Nope.  Not even close.  

In the end I had so many spots that I was unsure of that it just felt hopeless, so "Check Puzzle".

I had three errors, which feels about right.

I never, ever would have found those errors.

INTWOACTS.  

Ye gods, and little fishes.

After 30 minutes I revealed the whole puzzle and I still don’t understand many of the dramatic leaps taken. “Intwoacts?” I get it kind of but it’s a huge stretch. love a hard puzzle but this was as close to unworkable as they come.

There was a lot I really liked in this puzzle but several sections especially the north-west were incredibly frustrating as a non-American solver - seemingly random American secretary of defense from 13 years ago, random American gas brand, random American senator, random American college football reference and so on. 

But overall I'm still very thankful for yet another Thu-Fri-Sat combo with pre-2020 difficulty levels.

Also- big TIL moment for me that both the crime-related science and debate contests share the word "forensics" because they are rooted in the Latin word forensis, which means "of the forum" and is related to "speaking for judgement" in a public setting.

I find people who argue to be disagreeable.

But I really dislike FEUDISTS.
Not my favorite puzzle. Too many stretches and obscure references for my taste. 63 minutes of mostly waiting for the EPTs and ZARFSs to emerge from the fog.
Guesses and look-ups, one after another, and who knows, maybe I learned something, but it was more like a trip to the dentist than a crossword.

70 minutes of tooth grinding and lookups. If it weren't for my streak I'd have abandoned it.

A few good clues/solutions but far far too many obscurities

commentBody:
I have read others' complaints about setters' obscurantism with a certain quiet forebearance over the months because, after all, I had solved the puzzle.  

If I get the natick on a cross and am then able to look askance at and tiptoe past the unknown fill, what's it to me?  

I "got" it, too, in the end, didn't I?

But today I'm adding my voice to what I imagine will be the chorus.

This is your clue for UCLA?  

Why not clue instead its acreage or the year of its founding or the number of buildings on its campus?  I'd have loved to know the student enrollment in 1955, too!

Is it because "Back to the Future II" ranks right up there with "Casablanca" and "Dr Strangelove" in the pantheon of film for you, setidors?  Maybe I should give it another watch.

And who hasn't been sung to sleep on steamy summer nights in the outback by the sultry warble of the "Double-wattled cassowary?"  

Ah, the memories...before the dingo ate my baby, that is and when I still had a farm in East Africa.

And only yesterday I put in a bid on a lovely 17th century capote coat on Ebay.  Gee, I hope it's not too mothballed.

(to be continued...)

I’m glad for all of you who have been wanting a difficult puzzle, because you sure got one.

What I got was an exercise in frustration and futility. And after 
30+ minutes of lookups and still getting nowhere, I decided to cut my losses and bail. This is the first puzzle I have ever quit since I started doing them here, around 3 years ago, but if I’m not having fun I just can’t see the point.

I hope to have fun tomorrow.

Didn’t roll through this one but I welcomed the challenge. After all, it 👏 is 👏 Saturday! 👏 

Really liked the clues for SECOND STRINGERS, COVENS, and VIXENS.

TIL ZARFS.
This one seemed really tough. But somehow I beat my Saturday average by 20%. Therefore, I deem it a great puzzle.

The ineptitude of EPT irked. I believe the setter/editor were so pleased with themselves over MANE for “Trigger hair”  that the E absolutely had to be used. 

Took longer to get to the M in MÉLANGES than it should have. The rest of the puzzle was Saturdayesque. Perseverance was necessary. 

I didn’t know HAGEL, ZARFS, EVICO, WHOTEL, EMMET, nor that UCLA beat Washington in 1955 and will let them SEEP out of my brain with no regrets. NOVA is a bright new star in my world. I have no real clue what a CHIAPET is nor really care but I have to say all of them came out in the wash so just a hard puzzle. 

Not unfair.

I do not remember where I first encounter with "zarf, maybe junior high school, (hey I read a LOT of stuff back then.) Sometime I hoppe to see a related word, "finjan", which is a metal stand to hold a zarf. . My most favoraite word that is hard to find a place to use is "defenestrate", which I actually HAVE used. It doesn't require something unusual (like a zarf or a finjan), and tou can see what it means in a number of action movies...

That was a SATURDAY.  The journey:  confidence, shock, despair, dawning light, mounting victories, cloud of smug.  The cats were no help this morning so the victory is all mine.
TIL ZARF, which I will promptly forget.  If I want to add new words to the cerebral database, I must jettison some seventies song lyrics, and I am not willing to do that for zarf.
Constructor can zarf right off into the sun, to paraphrase John Oliver.

“And he have one, two, tree—oh! good many iron in him hide, too, Captain,” cried Queequeg disjointedly, “all twiske-tee be-twisk, like him—him—” faltering hard for a word, and screwing his hand round and round as though uncorking a bottle—“like him—him—”

“Corkscrew!” cried Ahab, “aye, Queequeg, the harpoons lie all twisted and wrenched in him...."

For some reason, this puzzle reminded me of Queequeg's comment about the irons in Moby Dick, all twiske-tee be-twisk.

For the record, I finished, and liked most of it. ZARFS not so much. EPT got a groan and a smile.

YMMV.

Don’t start with me!  I’M NOT IN THE MOOD

That would have been a great answer, except the clue doesn’t have quotation marks. Pay attention to punctuation! Or in this case, the lack thereof. SECOND STRINGER is a great entry. 

Excellent clues for VIXENS [They’re concerned with feeding kits] and COVENS [Spelling combinations?}. 

Thanks for the challenge, Byron.
I got the answer but I really don't understand the answer to 7up perhaps.

Well, this put me through the ringer!  I haven't had this much of a struggle with a Saturday puzzle IN FOREVER.  

There were some exceptional clues today.  [Trigger hair] for MANE was my all-time favorite, but [Don't start with me] for SECOND STRINGER, [Getting a break on  Broadway] for IN TWO ACTS, [7 up, perhaps] for EVEN STEVEN, and [They're concerned with feeding kits] for VIXENS were standouts as well.  I could live without FEUDISTS and SONARS, but they felt like a small price to pay for this throwback Saturday.

Well done, Byron! Thanks for a formidable challenge.

Been asking for a difficult one, and boy we sure got it! Now if we can just go one step further - difficult and clever. 

I want 'aha' moments, not obscurity. Especially when that obscurity is down and across in most of the puzzle.

I'm a relatively new solver (5 years), but the archived puzzles seem to be so much better.
Not fun. Honestly the type of crossword that makes me want to quit crosswords. Perhaps I’m just not as smart as the rest of you.

Oh, hello NYT Saturday puzzle!  Bringer of pauses that feel like they’ll never end … until a revelation hits that give you excitement you don’t feel anywhere else in the world.  Where you severely earn your squares and gloriously beam when you do.  Where you uncover an answer and go “Huh? … Wha?” until the wordplay hits you and you silently nod in awe.

Oh, hello Byron Walden, Saturday specialist, who takes you to that Moment of Truth, where you have to decide whether to cave or ditch, or soldier on.

Today’s opus features all the BW trademarks – stupendously low word and black square counts, wide range of fields, no-knows, word-wit, vagueness, cleanroom spotlessness, and amazing freshness.

I love aspects of every NYT puzzle, but I hang around for those special ones, those Capital-P Puzzles that take me away from the world and surround me with greatness.  Like every single one of yours, Byron.  After 25 years of making Times puzzles, your edge is as sharp as ever.  Thank you!

Whew!

I crawled, sweated, scraped and 
I may need a first aid kit.

Hubby may or may not have 
attempted to escape because
his wheelhouse is…
whatever isn’t mine,
and hoo-boy! 

I needed the help.

But, I do so love having to
spit out a little gravel from my teeth
after conquering a Saturday.

Next up?
Off to our favorite diner.
We have earned sausage gravy and biscuits.

Then the public market.

Ahhh…Saturday.
I sure do love you.
Very tough Saturday. Came in at over 6 minutes more than my average, and I most definitely needed some help. That northwest corner was a pit of despair for me. Ugh.
It was so nice to get a crossword that actually took effort. Had to go at this on in 8D!

Naticks to the left of them, naticks to the right of them, volleyed and thundered, but into the Valley of Dead Streaks road [sic] the 500 ... neurons, about as many as I could bring to bear on this puzzle last night. This morning, though, reinforcements arrived in large numbers to let me know that a chesterfield is not a BOAT, but a COAT; that a cassowary might not be two-RATTLED, but definitely two-WATTLED; that an operculum is not a LOD, but a LID; and so on and so forth!

This was a *glorious* way to end my week / start my weekend, and I'd like to buy Byron Walden a pack of his favorite beverage as a thank you, but, since I don't know where to ship it, this post will have to do. Thanks, Byron (and Will!!)

PS: Our podcast, Jean & Mike Do The New York Times Crossword, is still going strong: it's a short, fun, ad-free, family friendly discussion of  our TRAVAILS while solving the crossword, available wherever you get your podcasts.
Thanks, I hated it.

As always when I comment, I have to note my appreciation for our crossword creators and their mad skills in making these things in the first place.  And as usual when I comment, I have to quote (or in this case, paraphrase) Deb Amlen.

Today, my mileage varied.  

I’m all for offering varying degrees of difficulty throughout the week, but in my ideal world, the clues would always strike a balance between challenging and inaccessible.  Today’s crossed the line into the latter realm for me.  Some clues were totally lost on me, even when I got the answer, and my third scroll through the clues with very little fill reminded me of Einstein’s (?) definition of insanity.  Annoyance is generally not a good state of mind in which to solve a crossword.

I did, though, and will get over it and move on with my day.  Y’all have a lovely day and be kind to yourselves and one another.
Wow, even after sleeping on it, my performance was that of a double-wattled cassowary missing one of its wattles.

Bad puzzle

So I can finally give a real comment on this one.
Outside of MANE (because I oddly I just finished a crossword with almost the same clue) the only real entry I was sure of initially was VIXENS which, crossing with EXCISED finally opened the SE, despite my disbelief at ZARFS. In the SW I went back and forth between STREP and TSPS both of which seemed right, but once I figured out PODS that corner opened up. My PHONESHOME guess worked for the NE, thankfully. But the NW almost did me in. Had SAILS or TAILSOUT for the tennis clue, which led me to try LILYKIM for want of any other ideas, and nothing else was dropping for me. I was really almost ready to do a look up but then EPT finally came to me, which helped me get ROUNDEMUP, and finally the rest.
Overall this is “be careful what you wish for”. I appreciate, finally, a throwback Saturday with this level of difficulty and feel a great sense of accomplishment solving. On the other hand I have to agree this felt awfully forced even for a Walden puzzle. RIVERSEINE? Who says this, is there a gospel song about Paris? FEUDALISTS, really? LANDEDOUT just a little bit green painty? And I Iove 80s movie trivia as much as the next guy, or more, but the UCLA clue really was a stretch. Kudos for the SECONDSTRINGER clue though. I guess though I deserve these lumps for spouting off in recent weeks.

This one was unsolvable for me.  I was completely overmatched by ZARFS, the operculum clue (LID), ANDYKIM, trivia from a movie I only dimly remember seeing 30 years ago (UCLA), and others.  Often I can overcome gaps in my trivia knowledge through crosses, but not today, too many things that I simply didn't know without outside help.  After nearly two hours of frustration I gave up and looked at the answer key.
I feel strongly it crosses the bounds of crossword propriety to pair a clever misdirect clue -- “Folks who enjoy a well-aged beef?” —  with an obscure solution — FEUDISTS.

Well.. relieved to see that at least I'm not the only one who really struggled with this puzzle. Thirteen debut answers - don't recall seeing that before. Actually cheated a bit to get through it. I'll just leave it at that. And of course a puzzle find. A Tuesday from August 8, 2017 by Dan Flanagan. This on was all in the clues. Here are the theme answers: "4/4" COMMONTIME 11/11 VETERANSDAY 20/20 GREATVISION 50/50 EVENSTEVEN Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/8/2017&g=60&d=A I might put another puzzle find in a reply. ....
16D Someone you might yell at today?

One takeaway from today's comments:

Lots of people hate puzzles they can't solve.

When I saw BW's name on the puzzle, I knew it was going to be tough. But then, after a rough couple of passes, things started to break through. I conjured up a mental image of that torch on AMACO's logo, and another mental image of the CHIA PET, so I had high hopes. 

Loved it when the clues for MANE and VIXENS hit and made me smile. But then I hit the wall. I had DEBATE, but not CONTESTS. ZARFS, HAGEL, DISS, WATTLED and IN TWO ACTS were my undoing. And solving on paper doesn't give me the luxury of trying out letters until the right one magically appears. Finally, I just had to give up, because it was 5 a.m. and I still had a column and comments to read. I don't mind that the puzzle was tough. I don't mind that I blew it (well, yes, I do). I'm just happy that the NYT published a tough puzzle by a long-time constructor who's earned his stripes.
Anyone else try ASTARTE (fertility goddess AKA Ishtar) for the terracotta figurine? I was quite a few millennia too early, apparently.

That was brutal, the hardest puzzle in quite some time. I started it on Friday night but after not making much headway went back to it today. I somehow got it done but there were a lot of things I wasn't familiar with at all and it was just guess work.

This one goes out to all my fellow brain surgeon/Christmas carolers:

"O perculum, O perculum,
You insulate my insula"


("I'm wassailing away ... ")

Just for anyone who doesn't mind answering... if you can solve the Byron crosswords, what is your age? 

It's not just answers I've never heard before (KAYO, LANDED OUT, EPT - only in the upper left corner) I suspect I'm just kind of unable to step into his logic frequently enough.

Safe to say the peanut gallery is going to hate this one, as it put up a decent fight overall, and had a few spots that are likely to be *very* challenging for many/most solvers (myself included).

The spots I have in mind being:

- MANE / EPT
- LID / INFOREVER / ZARFS
- INTWOACTS / WATTLED

Those are all spots where I expect to hear complaints about how the puzzle was “impossible to solve without knowledge of arcane trivia”…and while I don’t have a problem with any of them (I enjoy gradually filling in trivia entries that are opaque to me, because, well, I actually enjoy solving crossword puzzles), I’ve gotta admit I had no clue if I’d be seeing the “good for you” splash screen when I entered the “E” in MANE.

Anyway, as soon as I saw the byline on today’s puzzle, I knew what I was in for: a fun solve with some stretchy/old-school entries. Thanks, Byron!

Beautiful, chewy Saturday.  Hard, but not too hard.
A lot to love and a lot to complain about.
But my complaints aren't "real" complaints, because I understand the constraints involved in making a good Saturday puzzle.  

On Saturday, I *want* some of the clues to upset me (third [!] sec'y of defense?), and I *want* a few words that I've never heard of before (like operculum and ZARF and NUUK).
And I should have known Cynthia ERIVO by now, but I still started with Cynthia Nixon.  (She's been in a bunch of things on B'way, right?)

My only "real" complaint is that PHONES HOME is too much of a gimme.  It was so obvious that I thought it had to be wrong, but when the crosses complied, I reluctantly put it in.

A great TIL with ZARFS.  Apparently everything from an ornate silver holder for your glass of tea to a cardboard cup sleeve from your favorite coffee house counts as a ZARF.

I hope I'll remember NUUK, but I hope it's not in the news too much in the next few years.
Operculum, on the other hand, I intend to forget immediately.

So, this is what I call an excellent Saturday puzzle.
Thanks, Byron Walden!

ZARFS?!?!?!

This puzzle was brutal, in the best possible way. My brain for the longest time did not want to accept ZARFS, though. I actually looked at the periodic table to check that there wasn't another four-letter element that ends in INC. (There's not.)

"Trigger hair" might be my favorite clue in a long time. I was insanely proud when I figured it out. I needed to wait for the coffee to kick in before I could remember NUUK, though.

This is my favorite puzzle of the year by far. Thank you, Byron Walden!
Thanks for the proper Saturday workout! I rather enjoyed the mix of wordplay and trivia.

Great challenge!
And man oh man, that NW corner had me stuck. But it was a mistake at 'double-Rattled' and 'IntRo acts' that added minutes to my solve
This was difficult! Very grateful for all the help from all of the people who provided so much info, tips and answers! Without them, I couldn't have finished this clever crossword!

Congratulations!
You solved a Saturday
Crossword in 1:36:22.

Most difficult puzzle for me INFOREVER---which was also my last entry and which took a very long time to finally see.

Still don't know EPT or ZARFS, and probably a few more things I entered. But solved this without any assistance.

I actually know what an operculum is, from long ago days collecting seashells. But I assumed it was simply a random word chosen from an old language, and confidently entered LAT.

One of so many missteps today. What a killer puzzle. Please, more at this level of difficulty on weekends!
now THAT'S what I call a Saturday puzzle!! Just fantastic.
Not feeling very ept. Oof.

Can anyone enlighten me what the answer UCLA has to do with Victor over Washington in Back to the Future and real life? I love the back to the future films, have watched them all dozens of times and can't make sense of this clue or answer. Thanks!

Wow, was this hard!  I had the UK, but had to cheat to get NUUK.  This cheat gave me the adorable EPT, which I never thought of and also the very clever MANE for "Trigger hair"  -- which I confused with hair-trigger and thought was something ready to explode like a temper.  But I then finished that section.

The section I couldn't finish -- mainly because there was no easy way to cheat -- was the ZARFS section.  I had ZARF and thought it had to be wrong, except it couldn't be because of the crosses which I was sure of.  WHAT THE BLEEP IS A ZARF AND WHAT DOES IT HAVE TO DO WITH A CUP????

I also wouldn't have gotten 30D in a million zillion years.  What on earth is a DISS track????   Even if I'd looked up the meaning of "operculum", it wouldn't have helped.

The rest I found do-able, but very, very hard.  Fiendish cluing everywhere.  I feel I should know the word "forensics" as it applies to DEBATEs, but I don't.  I can only apply it to autopsies and crime scenes.

One write-over: STRIPS before SPLITS for "takes off".

The kind of tough puzzle I love.  I might have loved it even more if I'd been able to finish the darn thing.
I have been on a massive roll on Crosswords for months now, but most of this one had me stumped. Nothing against the puzzle, but *oof*.
That was a workout!  I did not triumph smoothly over this puzzle.

I was just not on the right wavelength for this one. Even after solving I had to come to the comments to understand many of the clues (7 up, Trigger hair, Matches with forensics).
Most of my hour+ solve time was trying to figure out where I had gone wrong in the SE since ZARF couldn't possibly be anything. I came back to it with fresh eyes this morning and saw the real issue was HAGEr/WHOTEr. 

I did really enjoy the clue for EPT though.

Compare PHONESHOME and UCLA.  

The first is an oft-repeated, memorable part of a beloved, blockbuster movie.  The second is obscure trivia from a bad sequel.  

Even if Back to the Future II was someone's favorite film of all time, they might remember the plot included an almanac, the time-travelling existence of which was the MacGuffin from which comedy ensued.  But the game played that day?  And who was the victor?  It is the worst possible clue for an often used crossword answer.

Started out quite liking this one but became disappointed in the end. 

Positives first. Personal favourites were PHONES HOME, DEBATE CONTESTS, RIVER SEINE (Sunday Afternoon is one of my favourite art pieces) and IN FOREVER. I also loved being able to use my esoteric knowledge of world capitals for NUUK, and while I had to get all the crosses for ZARFS, I revel in coming across new words. 

That being said, there were too many clunky clues and entries for me to enjoy this one. LITTLE OL ME is fine I guess, but it seems odd to shorten one adjective and not the other. I've always used second string rather than SECOND STRINGER, and perhaps it's because I'm British but I don't understand the EVEN STEVEN cluing at all, even after googling. If a game is "7 up", I'd assume it means one team is 7 ahead. If both teams have 7 points, to me, that'd be "7 all". 

The clue for IN TWO ACTS also vexed me, as I don't see how the two are interchangable. Imagine asking "how is this play divided?" and receiving the answer "getting a break". Huh? I also fail to see how ROLLS is akin to "Triumphs smoothly"; I've never heard it used in this way, and after checking a few dictionaries, neither have they. Some more EPT clues would have been appreciated, or should I say apt, considering that's the word people actually use in this century, and Merriam Webster only defines EPT as "excess-profits tax". 

In sum, a potentially fun solve that has sadly been bogged down with inaccurate and inept cluing.

But for me this was hard. But it should have been average solve if my brain was working. But I loved it and I appreciated it.

I'm a relative newbie, but I completed this puzzle with the help of Mr. Google. Unfortunately, those cads, Mr. Check and Mr. Reveal CUT IN, but we didn't mind.

Back to Mr. Google, I found out some interesting things about 46A  [Setting for Seurat's "A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte"]. I knew the painting but I didn't know exactly where the scene was. Google explained not only that it's on the RIVERSEINE, but that it's an enormous painting measuring almost seven feet by ten feet. It took Seurat almost two years to paint. It is now located at the Art Institute of Chicago but a study for it can be seen at the Metropolitan Museum of Art. 

As many people know, the RIVERSEINE was sanitized for the recent Olympics there so athletes could swim in it. There is a lovely "piscine" (swimming pool) on a barge on the river, complete with restaurant, showers, etc. (Or at least, there used to be a piscine there.) A similar Parisian piscine barge is featured in the movie "The Life of Pi."

In the summertime, the banks of the Seine are covered with sand and Parisians go there with picnic baskets, beach umbrellas and Frisbees to enjoy the sun.

Forensics as crime-related science is a bit narrow in definition. Forensics is a legal term related to examining evidence and drawing conclusions. Forensic accounting is examining financial records to find evidence of criminal activity (e.g., fraud), while forensic art and reconstruction is related to the process of developing an image or representation of an individual from available evidence, my favorite example of which is sculpted models of persons based on skeletal data. There are other applications of the term in the legal world. So, a bit broader than just laboratory testing.
